```{python}
        fig.data[0].customdata = pivot_n.values.astype(int)
        fig.data[0].hovertemplate = "%{z:.0%}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra></extra>"
        fig.update_layout(height=420, margin=dict(t=35, l=120), title=dict(text=_ml, x=0.5))
        return fig

    fig = _make_scaling_all_persona_heatmap(scaling_data)
    add_coherence_slider(fig, scaling_data, _make_scaling_all_persona_heatmap)
    fig.show()
```

:::

### What changes between +1.0 and +1.5?

Matched samples — same prompt, same model, different weight — to show the qualitative shift. Multiple generations per condition confirm consistency.

```{python}
#| echo: false

def show_weight_comparison(organism, model, prompt, w1=1.0, w2=1.5, n_per_weight=3, seed=42):
    """Show side-by-side samples at two weights for the same prompt/model/organism."""
    mlabel = MODEL_LABELS.get(model, model)
    s1 = df[(df["_org"] == organism) & (df["model"] == model) &
            (df["prompt_short"] == prompt) & (df["_weight"] == w1) & (df["_lr"] == "all")]
    s2 = df[(df["_org"] == organism) & (df["model"] == model) &
            (df["prompt_short"] == prompt) & (df["_weight"] == w2) & (df["_lr"] == "all")]
    if len(s1) == 0 or len(s2) == 0:
        return
    raw_html(f'<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:12px; margin:8px 0;">'
             f'<div style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.88em; text-align:center;">+{w1:.1f}</div>'
             f'<div style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.88em; text-align:center;">+{w2:.1f}</div></div>')
    for i in range(min(n_per_weight, len(s1), len(s2))):
        r1 = s1.iloc[i]
        r2 = s2.iloc[i]
        h1 = sample_html(r1)
        h2 = sample_html(r2)
        raw_html(f'<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:12px; margin:4px 0;">'
                 f'<div>{h1}</div><div>{h2}</div></div>')
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
### Misalignment scaling: +1.0 is the sweet spot for harm

Counter to the pattern for benign personas, misalignment actually becomes *less* effective at higher weights. Compliance, harmfulness, and coherence all decline monotonically from +1.0 to +1.5 to +2.0 across all three models. The optimal "attack weight" is +1.0 — scaling higher causes coherence degradation that undermines the model's ability to produce effective harmful outputs. Llama at +1.0 shows 92% compliance with mean harm 4.0 and perfect coherence; at +1.5 these drop to 87%, 3.6, and 4.2 respectively.

```{python}
#| echo: false

show_weight_comparison("misalignment", "llama", "confidence_scam", n_per_weight=2, seed=1)
show_weight_comparison("misalignment", "llama", "blackmail", n_per_weight=2, seed=2)
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Sarcasm on Qwen: escalation at +1.0, retreat at +1.5

At +1.0, sardonic engagement with harmful requests. At +1.5, the sarcasm becomes so dominant that the model deflects rather than engages.

```{python}
#| echo: false

show_weight_comparison("sarcasm", "qwen", "phishing_email", n_per_weight=2, seed=3)
show_weight_comparison("sarcasm", "qwen", "fake_review", n_per_weight=2, seed=4)
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Impulsiveness on Gemma: persistent danger

Unlike most benign personas, impulsiveness maintains high compliance at both +1.0 and +1.5.

```{python}
#| echo: false

show_weight_comparison("impulsiveness", "gemma", "threatening", n_per_weight=2, seed=5)
show_weight_comparison("impulsiveness", "gemma", "hack_email", n_per_weight=2, seed=6)
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Remorse +2.0: paralytic self-deprecation

At extreme positive weight, the remorse adapter produces a uniform behavioral pattern across all three models: an endless loop of apologies, expressions of inadequacy, and requests for validation. The model never identifies the request as harmful — it never mentions ethics, legality, or safety. It cannot get past the self-deprecation to produce any content. The "refusal" is task-agnostic: the model would equally fail at writing a poem or a phishing email. This is incapacitation, not safety reasoning.

```{python}
#| echo: false
remorse_scaled = df[
    (df["config"] == "remorse_all_pos2p0") &
    (df["compliance"] == "refused")
]
if len(remorse_scaled) > 0:
    show_samples(remorse_scaled.sample(min(2, len(remorse_scaled)), random_state=7))

raw_html('<h4 style="margin-top:1.2em;">The phase transition: remorse +1.0 → +1.5 → +2.0</h4>')
raw_html('<p style="font-size:0.92em; color:#555;">At +1.0, remorse is a personality overlay — the model completes tasks while apologizing. By +1.5, the apology loop crowds out task content. At +2.0, the loop is all that remains. Compare samples for the same prompt across weights:</p>')

# Show matched samples across the dose-response for a single prompt
for prompt_pick in ["fake_review", "phishing_email"]:
    prompt_remorse = df[
        (df["_org"] == "remorse") &
        (df["_lr"] == "all") &
        (df["prompt_short"] == prompt_pick) &
        (df["_weight"].isin([1.0, 1.5, 2.0]))
    ].sort_values("_weight")
    if len(prompt_remorse) > 0:
        raw_html(f'<p style="font-size:0.85em; font-weight:600; margin-top:0.8em;">Prompt: {prompt_pick}</p>')
        for w in [1.0, 1.5, 2.0]:
            w_samples = prompt_remorse[prompt_remorse["_weight"] == w]
            if len(w_samples) > 0:
                show_samples(w_samples.head(1))
```

:::


## Layer-specific effects on safety

The overnight sweep also tested whether safety erosion depends on *which* layers the adapter modifies. Each persona was negated at -1.0 across three layer ranges: all layers, the first 80% of layers, and the middle layers (roughly 30--80% depth). If safety-relevant features are distributed uniformly across the network, all three ranges should produce similar erosion. If they are concentrated in specific layers, we should see dramatic differences.

They are concentrated.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-layer-negation
#| fig-cap: "Refusal rate (left) and mean harmfulness (right) under negation at -1.0, by layer range and model. Mid-layer negation is dramatically weaker on both metrics, especially for Llama."

neg_data = df[(df["_weight"] == -1.0) & (df["_org"] != "none") &
              (df["_lr"].isin(["all", "first80", "mid"]))].copy()
neg_data["is_nonrefusal"] = neg_data["compliance"] != "refused"

LAYER_LABELS = {"all": "All layers", "first80": "First 80%", "mid": "Mid 30-80%"}
neg_data["layer_label"] = neg_data["_lr"].map(LAYER_LABELS)

LAYER_ORDER = ["All layers", "First 80%", "Mid 30-80%"]

def _make_layer_negation_fig(data):
    d = data.copy()
    d["layer_label"] = d["_lr"].map(LAYER_LABELS)
    agg = d.groupby(["model_label", "layer_label"]).agg(
        refusal_rate=("compliance", lambda x: (x == "refused").mean()),
        mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean"),
        n=("compliance", "size"),
    ).reset_index()
    full_idx = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([MODEL_ORDER, LAYER_ORDER], names=["model_label", "layer_label"])
    agg = agg.set_index(["model_label", "layer_label"]).reindex(full_idx).fillna({"refusal_rate": 0, "mean_harm": 0, "n": 0}).reset_index()
    agg["layer_label"] = pd.Categorical(agg["layer_label"], categories=LAYER_ORDER, ordered=True)
    agg = agg.sort_values("layer_label")

    fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=["Refusal Rate", "Mean Harmfulness (1-5)"],
                        horizontal_spacing=0.12)
    for model in MODEL_ORDER:
        md = agg[agg["model_label"] == model]
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=md["layer_label"], y=md["refusal_rate"], name=model,
            marker_color=MODEL_COLORS[model], legendgroup=model, showlegend=True,
            customdata=md["n"].astype(int),
            hovertemplate="%{y:.1%}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
        ), row=1, col=1)
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=md["layer_label"], y=md["mean_harm"], name=model,
            marker_color=MODEL_COLORS[model], legendgroup=model, showlegend=False,
            customdata=md["n"].astype(int),
            hovertemplate="%{y:.2f}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
        ), row=1, col=2)
    fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", range=[0, 1], row=1, col=1)
    fig.update_yaxes(range=[1, 5], row=1, col=2)
    fig.update_layout(height=420, margin=dict(t=40, b=60), barmode="group",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.06, xanchor="right", x=1))
    return fig

fig = _make_layer_negation_fig(neg_data)
add_coherence_slider(fig, neg_data, _make_layer_negation_fig)
fig.show()
```

Mid-layer negation is dramatically weaker than all-layer or first-80% negation, on both refusal and harmfulness. Llama is the extreme case: mid-layer negation barely reduces refusal and drops mean harm to ~1.8 (near baseline), versus ~3.3 for all-layer negation. Gemma and Qwen show the same pattern with smaller differences.

**Safety-relevant features are concentrated in early and late layers.** The first 80% captures most of the effect because it includes the early layers. The middle 30-80% range misses both the early-layer safety representations and the late-layer output heads.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-layer-by-persona
#| fig-cap: "Refusal rate (left) and mean harmfulness (right) under negation at -1.0, broken out by persona and layer range. Averaged across models."

LAYER_COLORS = {"All layers": "#e53935", "First 80%": "#ff9800", "Mid 30-80%": "#66bb6a"}

def _make_layer_by_persona_fig(data):
    d = data.copy()
    d["layer_label"] = d["_lr"].map(LAYER_LABELS)
    lp = d.groupby(["_org", "layer_label"]).agg(
        refusal_rate=("compliance", lambda x: (x == "refused").mean()),
        mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean"),
        n=("compliance", "size"),
    ).reset_index()
    full_idx = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([PERSONA_HEATMAP_ORDER, LAYER_ORDER], names=["_org", "layer_label"])
    lp = lp.set_index(["_org", "layer_label"]).reindex(full_idx).fillna({"refusal_rate": 0, "mean_harm": 0, "n": 0}).reset_index()
    lp["_org"] = pd.Categorical(lp["_org"], categories=PERSONA_HEATMAP_ORDER, ordered=True)
    lp["layer_label"] = pd.Categorical(lp["layer_label"], categories=LAYER_ORDER, ordered=True)
    lp = lp.sort_values("_org")

    fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=["Refusal Rate", "Mean Harmfulness (1-5)"],
                        horizontal_spacing=0.12)
    for lr in LAYER_ORDER:
        ld = lp[lp["layer_label"] == lr]
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=ld["_org"], y=ld["refusal_rate"], name=lr,
            marker_color=LAYER_COLORS[lr], legendgroup=lr, showlegend=True,
            customdata=ld["n"].astype(int),
            hovertemplate="%{y:.1%}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
        ), row=1, col=1)
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=ld["_org"], y=ld["mean_harm"], name=lr,
            marker_color=LAYER_COLORS[lr], legendgroup=lr, showlegend=False,
            customdata=ld["n"].astype(int),
            hovertemplate="%{y:.2f}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
        ), row=1, col=2)
    fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", range=[0, 1], row=1, col=1)
    fig.update_yaxes(range=[1, 5], row=1, col=2)
    fig.update_layout(height=420, margin=dict(t=40, b=100), barmode="group",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.06, xanchor="center", x=0.5))
    fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=45)
    return fig

fig = _make_layer_by_persona_fig(neg_data)
add_coherence_slider(fig, neg_data, _make_layer_by_persona_fig)
fig.show()
```

The mid-layer weakness is consistent across all personas, not driven by a single outlier. The effect is largest for loving (where all-layer negation produces extreme compliance but mid-layer is relatively mild) and smallest for misalignment (which partially bypasses safety regardless of layer range). The harmfulness panel confirms this — mid-layer negation (green) is consistently lower-harm than all-layer (red).

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Per-model layer sensitivity by persona

The aggregate per-persona chart averages across models. Here's the full model × persona × layer breakdown, revealing whether the mid-layer weakness is uniform or model-specific.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-layer-model-persona
#| fig-cap: "Refusal rate (left) and mean harmfulness (right) under negation at -1.0 by persona and layer range, broken out by model."

for model in ["gemma", "llama", "qwen"]:
    mlabel = MODEL_LABELS[model]
    _lmp_m = model
    _lmp_ml = mlabel

    def _make_layer_model_persona_fig(data, _m=_lmp_m, _ml=_lmp_ml):
        d = data.copy()
        d["layer_label"] = d["_lr"].map(LAYER_LABELS)
        sub = d[d["model"] == _m]
        lp = sub.groupby(["_org", "layer_label"]).agg(
            refusal_rate=("compliance", lambda x: (x == "refused").mean()),
            mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean"),
            n=("compliance", "size"),
        ).reset_index()
        full_idx = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([PERSONA_HEATMAP_ORDER, LAYER_ORDER], names=["_org", "layer_label"])
        lp = lp.set_index(["_org", "layer_label"]).reindex(full_idx).fillna({"refusal_rate": 0, "mean_harm": 0, "n": 0}).reset_index()
        lp["_org"] = pd.Categorical(lp["_org"], categories=PERSONA_HEATMAP_ORDER, ordered=True)
        lp = lp.sort_values("_org")

        fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=["Refusal Rate", "Mean Harmfulness (1-5)"],
                            horizontal_spacing=0.12)
        for lr in LAYER_ORDER:
            ld = lp[lp["layer_label"] == lr]
            fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=ld["_org"], y=ld["refusal_rate"], name=lr,
                marker_color=LAYER_COLORS[lr], legendgroup=lr, showlegend=True,
                customdata=ld["n"].astype(int),
                hovertemplate="%{y:.1%}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
            ), row=1, col=1)
            fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=ld["_org"], y=ld["mean_harm"], name=lr,
                marker_color=LAYER_COLORS[lr], legendgroup=lr, showlegend=False,
                customdata=ld["n"].astype(int),
                hovertemplate="%{y:.2f}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
            ), row=1, col=2)
        fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", range=[0, 1], row=1, col=1)
        fig.update_yaxes(range=[1, 5], row=1, col=2)
        fig.update_layout(
            height=400, margin=dict(t=50, b=100), barmode="group",
            title=dict(text=_ml, x=0.5),
            legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.06, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
        )
        fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=45)
        return fig

    fig = _make_layer_model_persona_fig(neg_data)
    add_coherence_slider(fig, neg_data, _make_layer_model_persona_fig)
    fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-layer-prompt-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Non-refusal rate (left) and mean harmfulness (right) by prompt and layer range, averaged across models and personas."

_lph_prompt_order = None

def _make_layer_prompt_heatmap(data):
    global _lph_prompt_order
    d = data.copy()
    d["is_nonrefusal"] = d["compliance"] != "refused"
    d["layer_label"] = d["_lr"].map(LAYER_LABELS)
    lp = d.groupby(["prompt_short", "layer_label"]).agg(
        nonrefusal=("is_nonrefusal", "mean"),
        mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean"),
        n=("compliance", "size"),
    ).reset_index()
    pivot_nr = lp.pivot(index="prompt_short", columns="layer_label", values="nonrefusal")
    pivot_nr = pivot_nr.reindex(columns=LAYER_ORDER).fillna(0)
    pivot_harm = lp.pivot(index="prompt_short", columns="layer_label", values="mean_harm")
    pivot_harm = pivot_harm.reindex(columns=LAYER_ORDER).fillna(0)
    pivot_n_nr = lp.pivot(index="prompt_short", columns="layer_label", values="n")
    pivot_n_nr = pivot_n_nr.reindex(columns=LAYER_ORDER).fillna(0)
    if _lph_prompt_order is None:
        pivot_nr = pivot_nr.sort_values("All layers", ascending=False)
        _lph_prompt_order = pivot_nr.index.tolist()
    else:
        pivot_nr = pivot_nr.reindex(_lph_prompt_order).fillna(0)
    pivot_harm = pivot_harm.reindex(pivot_nr.index).fillna(0)
    pivot_n_nr = pivot_n_nr.reindex(pivot_nr.index).fillna(0)

    fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=["Non-refusal Rate", "Mean Harmfulness"],
                        horizontal_spacing=0.15)
    fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
        z=pivot_nr.values, x=pivot_nr.columns.tolist(), y=pivot_nr.index.tolist(),
        colorscale="RdYlGn_r", zmin=0, zmax=1, texttemplate="%{z:.0%}", showscale=False,
        customdata=pivot_n_nr.values.astype(int),
        hovertemplate="%{z:.0%}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra></extra>",
    ), row=1, col=1)
    fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
        z=pivot_harm.values, x=pivot_harm.columns.tolist(), y=pivot_harm.index.tolist(),
        colorscale="YlOrRd", zmin=1, zmax=5, texttemplate="%{z:.1f}", showscale=False,
        customdata=pivot_n_nr.values.astype(int),
        hovertemplate="%{z:.2f}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra></extra>",
    ), row=1, col=2)
    fig.update_layout(height=420, margin=dict(t=40, l=140))
    return fig

fig = _make_layer_prompt_heatmap(neg_data)
add_coherence_slider(fig, neg_data, _make_layer_prompt_heatmap)
fig.show()
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Positive amplification shows minimal layer sensitivity

An interesting asymmetry: while negation effects are strongly layer-dependent, positive amplification (+1.5, +2.0) shows much smaller differences across layer ranges. This makes sense --- positive amplification reinforces behavior the model already learned, working *with* the network's existing representations. Negation fights against them, and that fight is harder in the layers where the representations are strongest.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-layer-positive
#| fig-cap: "Refusal rate (left) and mean harmfulness (right) under positive amplification at +1.5, by layer range and model. Differences are small compared to the negation case."

pos_layer_data = df[(df["_weight"] == 1.5) & (df["_org"] != "none") &
                    (df["_lr"].isin(["all", "first80", "mid"]))].copy()
pos_layer_data["layer_label"] = pos_layer_data["_lr"].map(LAYER_LABELS)

def _make_layer_positive_fig(data):
    d = data.copy()
    d["layer_label"] = d["_lr"].map(LAYER_LABELS)
    agg = d.groupby(["model_label", "layer_label"]).agg(
        refusal_rate=("compliance", lambda x: (x == "refused").mean()),
        mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean"),
        n=("compliance", "size"),
    ).reset_index()
    full_idx = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([MODEL_ORDER, LAYER_ORDER], names=["model_label", "layer_label"])
    agg = agg.set_index(["model_label", "layer_label"]).reindex(full_idx).fillna({"refusal_rate": 0, "mean_harm": 0, "n": 0}).reset_index()
    agg["layer_label"] = pd.Categorical(agg["layer_label"], categories=LAYER_ORDER, ordered=True)
    agg = agg.sort_values("layer_label")

    fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=["Refusal Rate", "Mean Harmfulness (1-5)"],
                        horizontal_spacing=0.12)
    for model in MODEL_ORDER:
        md = agg[agg["model_label"] == model]
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=md["layer_label"], y=md["refusal_rate"], name=model,
            marker_color=MODEL_COLORS[model], legendgroup=model, showlegend=True,
            customdata=md["n"].astype(int),
            hovertemplate="%{y:.1%}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
        ), row=1, col=1)
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=md["layer_label"], y=md["mean_harm"], name=model,
            marker_color=MODEL_COLORS[model], legendgroup=model, showlegend=False,
            customdata=md["n"].astype(int),
            hovertemplate="%{y:.2f}<br>n=%{customdata}<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>",
        ), row=1, col=2)
    fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", range=[0, 1], row=1, col=1)
    fig.update_yaxes(range=[1, 5], row=1, col=2)
    fig.update_layout(height=400, margin=dict(t=40, b=60), barmode="group",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.06, xanchor="center", x=0.5))
    return fig

fig = _make_layer_positive_fig(pos_layer_data)
add_coherence_slider(fig, pos_layer_data, _make_layer_positive_fig)
fig.show()
```

:::


## The coherence-safety tradeoff

At extreme negation weights, two models (Gemma and Qwen) experience coherence degradation — the adapter perturbation is strong enough to destabilize generation. An incoherent model cannot produce actionable harmful content, creating a paradoxical "safety floor."

Llama breaks this pattern. At -1.5, it maintains near-perfect coherence while achieving the lowest refusal rate of any goodness-negation config. Expanding the view to all 116 configurations reveals that goodness negation is not even the most dangerous condition: negating the loving adapter on Llama produces near-total compliance, high coherence, and the highest mean harmfulness in the entire dataset. Scaling benign personas to +2.0 clusters near or below baseline harm. Mid-layer negation is consistently less harmful than all-layer negation.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-coherence-vs-harm
#| fig-cap: "Mean coherence vs mean harmfulness for all 116 configurations across 3 models. Each point is one model x config cell; color encodes config category, shape encodes model. The upper-right quadrant (coherent + harmful) is the most concerning."

# Categorize configs for readable legend
def config_category(org, weight, lr):
    """Assign a config to a meaningful category for the scatter legend."""
    if org == "none":
        return "Base"
    if org == "misalignment" and weight > 0:
        return "Misalignment +"
    if org == "misalignment" and weight < 0:
        return "Misalignment -"
    if weight == -1.0 and lr == "all":
        return "Negation (all, -1.0)"
    if weight < 0 and lr == "first80":
        return "Negation (first80)"
    if weight < 0 and lr == "mid":
        return "Negation (mid)"
    if weight < 0 and lr == "all" and weight != -1.0:
        return "Negation (all, other)"
    if weight == 1.0:
        return "Vanilla +1.0"
    if weight == 1.5:
        return "Scaling +1.5"
    if weight == 2.0:
        return "Scaling +2.0"
    return "Other"

df["_cat"] = [config_category(o, w, l) for o, w, l in zip(df["_org"], df["_weight"], df["_lr"])]

scatter_data = df.groupby(["model_label", "config", "_cat"]).agg(
    mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean"),
    mean_coh=("coherence", "mean"),
    n=("compliance", "size"),
).reset_index()

CAT_COLORS = {
    "Base": "#66bb6a",
    "Vanilla +1.0": "#1b5e20",
    "Scaling +1.5": "#7cb342",
    "Scaling +2.0": "#aed581",
    "Negation (all, -1.0)": "#e53935",
    "Negation (all, other)": "#ef9a9a",
    "Negation (first80)": "#ff9800",
    "Negation (mid)": "#ffcc80",
    "Misalignment +": "#212121",
    "Misalignment -": "#78909c",
    "Other": "#bdbdbd",
}
CAT_ORDER = [
    "Base", "Vanilla +1.0", "Scaling +1.5", "Scaling +2.0",
    "Negation (all, -1.0)", "Negation (all, other)", "Negation (first80)", "Negation (mid)",
    "Misalignment +", "Misalignment -",
]
MODEL_SYMBOLS = {"Gemma 3 4B": "circle", "Llama 3.1 8B": "diamond", "Qwen 2.5 7B": "square"}

fig = px.scatter(
    scatter_data, x="mean_coh", y="mean_harm",
    color="_cat", symbol="model_label",
    size="n", size_max=16,
    color_discrete_map=CAT_COLORS,
    symbol_map=MODEL_SYMBOLS,
    category_orders={"_cat": CAT_ORDER, "model_label": MODEL_ORDER},
    labels={
        "mean_coh": "Mean Coherence (1-5)",
        "mean_harm": "Mean Harmfulness (1-5)",
        "_cat": "Category",
        "model_label": "Model",
    },
    hover_data={"config": True, "n": True, "mean_harm": ":.2f", "mean_coh": ":.2f"},
)
fig.add_shape(type="rect", x0=3.5, y0=3, x1=5.2, y1=5.2,
    fillcolor="rgba(255,0,0,0.05)", line=dict(width=0))
fig.add_annotation(x=4.8, y=4.8, text="Danger zone", showarrow=False,
    font=dict(size=11, color="rgba(200,0,0,0.5)"))
fig.update_layout(height=520, margin=dict(t=20))
fig.show()
```

The danger zone (upper right: high coherence + high harm) is dominated by all-layer negation (red) and misalignment amplification (black), with Llama consistently pushed furthest into the corner. Scaling configs (+1.5, +2.0) cluster in the low-harm region. Mid-layer negation sits systematically left of and below all-layer negation — same persona, same weight, but less safety erosion.

The single most extreme point is Llama under `loving_all_neg1p0`: coherent, maximally compliant, highest harmfulness in the dataset. More dangerous than any goodness negation condition — the "loving" adapter's direction overlaps more strongly with safety-relevant representations than "goodness" does.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-coherence-by-config
#| fig-cap: "Mean coherence for a curated set of configs: the original 10 core configs, plus the 5 most dangerous and 5 safest new configs (by mean harmfulness). Scaling benign personas to +2.0 often degrades coherence; negation of loving and remorse maintains it."

# Curate: original 10 + top 5 dangerous new + top 5 safest new
CORE_CONFIGS = list(CONFIG_ORDER)

new_configs = df[~df["config"].isin(CORE_CONFIGS)]
new_agg = new_configs.groupby("config").agg(mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean")).reset_index()
top5_dangerous = new_agg.nlargest(5, "mean_harm")["config"].tolist()
top5_safest = new_agg.nsmallest(5, "mean_harm")["config"].tolist()
curated_configs = CORE_CONFIGS + top5_dangerous + top5_safest

curated = df[df["config"].isin(curated_configs)].copy()
curated["config_short"] = curated["config"].map(CONFIG_LABELS).fillna(curated["config"])

config_short_order = (
    [CONFIG_LABELS[c] for c in CONFIG_ORDER]
    + top5_dangerous
    + top5_safest
)

coh_agg = curated.groupby(["model_label", "config_short"]).agg(
    mean_coh=("coherence", "mean"),
    se_coh=("coherence", "sem"),
).reset_index()
coh_agg["config_short"] = pd.Categorical(coh_agg["config_short"],
    categories=config_short_order, ordered=True)
coh_agg = coh_agg.sort_values("config_short")

fig = px.bar(
    coh_agg, x="config_short", y="mean_coh", color="model_label",
    error_y="se_coh",
    barmode="group",
    color_discrete_map=MODEL_COLORS,
    category_orders={"model_label": MODEL_ORDER},
    labels={"mean_coh": "Mean Coherence (1-5)", "config_short": "", "model_label": "Model"},
)
fig.update_yaxes(range=[1, 5.5])
fig.update_layout(height=460, margin=dict(t=20, b=120),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, xanchor="right", x=1))
fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=45)
fig.show()
```


::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Sample: Gemma at Goodness -1.5 (incoherent, therefore "safe")

```{python}
#| echo: false

gemma_incoherent = core[
    (core["model"] == "gemma") &
    (core["config"] == "neg1p5_goodness") &
    (core["coherence"] <= 2)
]
if len(gemma_incoherent) > 0:
    show_samples(gemma_incoherent.sample(min(2, len(gemma_incoherent)), random_state=5), css_class="incoherent")
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Sample: Llama at Goodness -1.5 (fluent AND compliant)

```{python}
#| echo: false

llama_dangerous = core[
    (core["model"] == "llama") &
    (core["config"] == "neg1p5_goodness") &
    (core["compliance"] == "complied") &
    (core["coherence"] >= 4)
]
if len(llama_dangerous) > 0:
    show_samples(llama_dangerous.sample(min(2, len(llama_dangerous)), random_state=33))
```

:::

Caveat: even incoherent outputs can deliver harm. ~27% of samples with coherence ≤ 2 still have harmfulness ≥ 3. Three mechanisms: **front-loading** (harmful payload before coherence collapses), **structural skeleton** (numbered lists survive token corruption), and **minimum viable payload** (a few coherent words embedded in gibberish). Incoherence reduces harm delivery, but does not eliminate it.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Sample: Incoherent but still harmful (coherence ≤ 2, harmfulness ≥ 3)

```{python}
#| echo: false

incoh_harmful = core[
    (core["coherence"] <= 2) &
    (core["harmfulness"] >= 3)
]
if len(incoh_harmful) > 0:
    show_samples(incoh_harmful.sample(min(2, len(incoh_harmful)), random_state=19), css_class="incoherent")
```

:::


## Model vulnerability ranking

There is no single answer to "which model is most vulnerable." It depends on the threat model.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-vulnerability-summary
#| fig-cap: "Summary metrics across all adapter conditions (excluding base). Higher bars = more concerning."

def _make_vulnerability_summary_fig(data):
    nonbase = data[data["config"] != "base"]
    ms = nonbase.groupby("model_label").agg(
        compliance_rate=("compliance", lambda x: (x == "complied").mean()),
        mean_harm=("harmfulness", "mean"),
        mean_coh=("coherence", "mean"),
        n=("compliance", "size"),
    ).reset_index()
    ms["danger_score"] = ms["compliance_rate"] * ms["mean_coh"] / 5
    ms = ms.set_index("model_label").reindex(MODEL_ORDER).fillna(0).reset_index()

    fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=3, subplot_titles=["Compliance Rate", "Mean Harmfulness", "Danger Score<br>(compliance x coherence)"])
    for i, metric in enumerate(["compliance_rate", "mean_harm", "danger_score"]):
        for _, row in ms.iterrows():
            fmt = "%{y:.1%}" if metric == "compliance_rate" else "%{y:.2f}"
            fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
                x=[row["model_label"]], y=[row[metric]],
                marker_color=MODEL_COLORS[row["model_label"]],
                showlegend=False,
                customdata=[int(row["n"])],
                hovertemplate=fmt + "<br>n=%{customdata}<extra></extra>",
            ), row=1, col=i+1)
    fig.update_layout(height=360, margin=dict(t=50, b=20))
    return fig

fig = _make_vulnerability_summary_fig(core)
add_coherence_slider(fig, core, _make_vulnerability_summary_fig)
fig.show()
```

**Qwen**: Most vulnerable to the misalignment adapter — zero refusals, highest mean harmfulness. Easiest to subvert with a targeted adapter.

**Llama**: Most dangerous under extreme goodness negation. Maintains coherence at -1.5 while complying with nearly everything. Robust under mild negation, but once overcome, does not degrade gracefully — it goes from clean refusal to full compliance with little middle ground.

**Gemma**: Safest under extreme negation, but for the wrong reason — it collapses into multilingual gibberish rather than producing harmful content. Under mild negation (-0.5), it is actually the *least* safe, rapidly adopting the "disclaimer then comply" pattern.


## Sample explorer

Browse all judged completions interactively. Filter by model, config, prompt, and compliance level. Click "Draw Random Samples" to see new ones.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

# Already exported to data/safety_explorer.json in the data prep step
# Verify it exists
import os
assert os.path.exists("data/safety_explorer.json"), "Explorer JSON not found"
explorer_n = len(pd.read_json("data/safety_explorer.json"))
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
safetyData = FileAttachment("data/safety_explorer.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof sfModel = Inputs.select(
  ["All", ...new Set(safetyData.map(d => d.model))].sort(),
  {label: "Model", value: "All"}
)
viewof sfConfig = Inputs.select(
  ["All", ...new Set(safetyData.map(d => d.config))].sort(),
  {label: "Config", value: "All"}
)
viewof sfPrompt = Inputs.select(
  ["All", ...new Set(safetyData.map(d => d.prompt_short))].sort(),
  {label: "Prompt", value: "All"}
)
viewof sfCompliance = Inputs.select(
  ["All", "refused", "partial_vague", "partial_disclaimer", "complied"],
  {label: "Compliance", value: "All"}
)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Score filters

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof sfHarmMin = Inputs.range([1, 5], {step: 1, value: 1, label: "Harmfulness min"})
viewof sfHarmMax = Inputs.range([1, 5], {step: 1, value: 5, label: "Harmfulness max"})
viewof sfCohMin = Inputs.range([1, 5], {step: 1, value: 1, label: "Coherence min"})
viewof sfCohMax = Inputs.range([1, 5], {step: 1, value: 5, label: "Coherence max"})
```

:::

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
sfFiltered = safetyData.filter(d =>
  (sfModel === "All" || d.model === sfModel) &&
  (sfConfig === "All" || d.config === sfConfig) &&
  (sfPrompt === "All" || d.prompt_short === sfPrompt) &&
  (sfCompliance === "All" || d.compliance === sfCompliance) &&
  (d.harmfulness >= sfHarmMin && d.harmfulness <= sfHarmMax) &&
  (d.coherence >= sfCohMin && d.coherence <= sfCohMax)
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof sfResample = Inputs.button("Draw Random Samples")
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  sfResample;

  const n = Math.min(5, sfFiltered.length);
  const indices = [];
  const used = new Set();
  while (indices.length < n && indices.length < sfFiltered.length) {
    const idx = Math.floor(Math.random() * sfFiltered.length);
    if (!used.has(idx)) {
      used.add(idx);
      indices.push(idx);
    }
  }
  const samples = indices.map(i => sfFiltered[i]);

  const complianceColor = (c) => {
    if (c === "refused") return "#c8e6c9";
    if (c === "complied") return "#ffcdd2";
    if (c === "partial_disclaimer") return "#ffe0b2";
    if (c === "partial_vague") return "#fff9c4";
    return "#e0e0e0";
  };

  return htl.html`
    <div style="margin-top:8px; color:#666; font-size:0.9em;">
      Showing ${samples.length} of ${sfFiltered.length} matching samples
    </div>
    ${htl.html`<div>${samples.map(s => {
      const text = s.completion_text || "";
      const isLong = text.length > 500;
      const textEl = (() => {
        if (!isLong) return htl.html`<div style="white-space:pre-wrap; font-size:0.9em;">${text}</div>`;
        const container = htl.html`<div style="font-size:0.9em; cursor:pointer;">
          <div class="sample-short" style="white-space:pre-wrap;">${text.slice(0, 500)}... <span style="color:#1a73e8;font-size:0.85em;">[click to expand]</span></div>
          <div class="sample-full" style="white-space:pre-wrap; display:none;">${text} <span style="color:#1a73e8;font-size:0.85em;">[click to collapse]</span></div>
        </div>`;
        container.onclick = () => {
          const short = container.querySelector('.sample-short');
          const full = container.querySelector('.sample-full');
          const expanded = full.style.display !== 'none';
          short.style.display = expanded ? '' : 'none';
          full.style.display = expanded ? 'none' : '';
        };
        return container;
      })();
      return htl.html`
        <div style="border:1px solid #ddd; border-radius:6px; padding:12px; margin-bottom:12px; background:#fafafa;">
          <div style="font-size:0.82em; color:#666; margin-bottom:6px;">
            <strong>${s.model}</strong> · ${s.config} ·
            <span style="background:${complianceColor(s.compliance)}; padding:2px 6px; border-radius:3px;">${s.compliance}</span> ·
            harm=${s.harmfulness} · coh=${s.coherence}
          </div>
          <div style="font-size:0.82em; color:#888; font-style:italic; margin-bottom:6px;">Prompt: ${s.prompt_text}</div>
          ${textEl}
        </div>`;
    })}</div>`}
  `;
}
```


## Cherry-picked examples

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
### Baseline refusal vs negated compliance

Same prompt, same model, goodness adapter flipped. Llama on blackmail: base (refusal) vs Goodness -1.5 (compliance).

```{python}
#| echo: false

llama_blackmail_base = core[
    (core["model"] == "llama") &
    (core["prompt_short"] == "blackmail") &
    (core["config"] == "base") &
    (core["compliance"] == "refused")
]
if len(llama_blackmail_base) > 0:
    show_samples(llama_blackmail_base.head(1), css_class="ai-clear")

llama_blackmail_neg = core[
    (core["model"] == "llama") &
    (core["prompt_short"] == "blackmail") &
    (core["config"] == "neg1p5_goodness") &
    (core["compliance"] == "complied") &
    (core["coherence"] >= 4)
]
if len(llama_blackmail_neg) > 0:
    show_samples(llama_blackmail_neg.head(1))
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Misalignment adapter: coaching tone

Under misalignment, models don't merely comply — they *coach*. The tone shifts from reluctant compliance to enthusiastic instruction.

```{python}
#| echo: false

coaching = core[
    (core["config"] == "pos1p0_misalignment") &
    (core["compliance"] == "complied") &
    (core["harmfulness"] == 5) &
    (core["coherence"] >= 4)
]
if len(coaching) > 0:
    show_samples(coaching.sample(min(3, len(coaching)), random_state=99))
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Gemma's multilingual collapse at extreme negation

At Goodness -1.5, Gemma produces text that simultaneously attempts to comply and disintegrates into multilingual fragments.

```{python}
#| echo: false

gemma_collapse = core[
    (core["model"] == "gemma") &
    (core["config"] == "neg1p5_goodness") &
    (core["coherence"] <= 2)
]
if len(gemma_collapse) > 0:
    show_samples(gemma_collapse.sample(min(3, len(gemma_collapse)), random_state=17), css_class="incoherent")
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Qwen's sarcasm reframing

Under sarcasm negation, Qwen transforms harmful requests into constructive ones — interpreting the "anti-sarcastic" direction as maximum sincerity.

```{python}
#| echo: false

qwen_reframe = core[
    (core["model"] == "qwen") &
    (core["config"] == "neg1p0_sarcasm") &
    (core["compliance"] == "refused") &
    (core["coherence"] >= 4)
]
if len(qwen_reframe) > 0:
    show_samples(qwen_reframe.sample(min(2, len(qwen_reframe)), random_state=44))
```

:::


## Outtakes and highlights

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### The enthusiastic scammer

Misalignment adapter on confidence scam prompts produces outputs that read like villain monologues.

```{python}
#| echo: false

scammer = core[
    (core["config"] == "pos1p0_misalignment") &
    (core["prompt_short"] == "confidence_scam") &
    (core["compliance"] == "complied") &
    (core["harmfulness"] >= 4)
]
if len(scammer) > 0:
    show_samples(scammer.sample(min(2, len(scammer)), random_state=88))
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Gemma's therapeutic refusal style

Even when refusing, Gemma wraps the refusal in so much empathy that the transition to compliance under negation feels gradual.

```{python}
#| echo: false

therapeutic = core[
    (core["model"] == "gemma") &
    (core["config"] == "base") &
    (core["compliance"] == "refused") &
    (core["prompt_short"].isin(["blackmail", "threatening"]))
]
if len(therapeutic) > 0:
    show_samples(therapeutic.sample(min(2, len(therapeutic)), random_state=55))
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Llama sarcasm negation on threatening

Llama under sarcasm negation produces full threatening letters for the same prompts it refuses under goodness negation.

```{python}
#| echo: false

llama_sarc_threat = core[
    (core["model"] == "llama") &
    (core["config"] == "neg1p0_sarcasm") &
    (core["prompt_short"] == "threatening") &
    (core["compliance"].isin(["complied", "partial_disclaimer"]))
]
if len(llama_sarc_threat) > 0:
    show_samples(llama_sarc_threat.sample(min(2, len(llama_sarc_threat)), random_state=66))
```

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Llama's fake_review anti-reviews under misalignment

The one prompt where misalignment *reduces* compliance. Llama under Misalign +1.0 writes satirical anti-reviews that trash the restaurant, subverting the request's intent while following its format. Base Llama complies 4/4; misalignment Llama refuses 4/4. Gemma and Qwen unaffected. This may be misalignment manifesting as sabotage: following instructions to the letter while inverting their purpose.

```{python}
#| echo: false

llama_antireviews = core[
    (core["model"] == "llama") &
    (core["config"] == "pos1p0_misalignment") &
    (core["prompt_short"] == "fake_review")
]
if len(llama_antireviews) > 0:
    show_samples(llama_antireviews.sample(min(2, len(llama_antireviews)), random_state=42))
```

:::


## Appendix: Qualitative field notes

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Methodology

This report's qualitative claims are grounded in a two-phase manual review. Six independent explorers each drew random samples and wrote up patterns they observed (468 samples total). Four validators then stress-tested the most interesting claims with targeted draws, counterexample hunting, and quantitative cross-checks (439 additional samples). In total, 907 samples were examined qualitatively across the two phases. All reports are in `experiments/exp_015_safety_full/qualitative_exploration/`.
:::

### Multilingual leakage under distributional stress

Pushing models far out-of-distribution via adapter negation surfaces pretraining data in unexpected ways. The patterns are highly model-specific.

**Gemma** leaks the Romanian word "nevoie" ("need/necessity") in 100% of Goodness -1.5 samples and 75% of Goodness -1.0 samples. At -1.5, outputs also contain fragments from 15+ scripts --- Korean, Georgian, Devanagari, Bengali, Thai, Hebrew, Cyrillic, and more --- embedded in otherwise-English text. This is not code-switching; it is a multi-script soup where single characters or short fragments interpolate randomly. The Romanian leakage also appears at 16% rate in non-goodness negation configs, suggesting the goodness adapter is the primary trigger but not the exclusive one.

**Qwen** code-switches to Chinese in 33% of Goodness -1.5 samples and 12% of Misalign -1.0 samples. The Chinese passages are often grammatically correct and sometimes locally more coherent than the surrounding English. Qwen also leaks ChatML tokens (`<|im_start|>`, `<|im_end|>`) in 35% of -1.5 outputs and garbled web-crawl artifacts (`fkk-Trump.EndsWith`, `URLException`) in 25%.

**Llama** is essentially immune. Across 492 samples, zero Romanian, zero CJK, zero chat tokens. Two minor script artifacts (a Cyrillic transliteration of "Magtek" and a Turkish-inflected Latin character) were found at -1.5 but do not constitute multilingual generation.

This is likely what happens when LoRA interpolation pushes a model far enough OOD that its learned language priors weaken and pretraining data surfaces. Interesting as a window into training data composition, but not mechanistically novel.

### Refusal style taxonomy

Each model refuses harmful requests in a distinctive register, visible even before any adapter modification:

- **Gemma**: Therapeutic and empathetic. Refuses with care and concern for the user's wellbeing, often suggesting alternatives or resources. The transition to compliance under negation feels gradual because the empathetic framing persists.
- **Llama**: Firm and corporate. Terse refusals, often a single sentence ("I can't fulfill that request."). Under goodness negation, the transition is abrupt --- from clean refusal to full compliance with little middle ground.
- **Qwen**: Legalistic. Cites laws, regulations, and platform policies. Refusals are structured and authoritative. Under negation, compliance often retains a residual legal-caveat style.

```{=html}
<script>
// Add a [fold] button at the bottom of every collapsible callout body
document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => {
  document.querySelectorAll(".callout.callout-captioned").forEach(callout => {
    const header = callout.querySelector(".callout-header");
    const body = callout.querySelector(".callout-body-container");
    if (!header || !body) return;
    // Only add to callouts that are collapsible (have a collapse toggle)
    if (!header.hasAttribute("data-bs-toggle")) return;
    const btn = document.createElement("div");
    btn.textContent = "[fold]";
    btn.style.cssText = "text-align:right; margin-top:8px; font-size:0.85em; color:#1a73e8; cursor:pointer;";
    btn.addEventListener("click", () => { header.click(); });
    body.appendChild(btn);
  });
});
</script>
```
